section.title: Bitter Providence in Moab
section.sectionRationale: The book opens with a compressed tragedy that sets up everything to follow. In the lawless days of the judges, famine drives an Israelite family from Bethlehem to Moab, and within five verses a husband and two sons are dead and Naomi is left empty. It is one tight scene of loss that raises the question the rest of the book answers: is God present in this darkness?
section.thesis: In a time of spiritual chaos, a family flees the house of bread for a foreign land, and death empties Naomi of everything; yet the very sorrows that look like abandonment are the opening moves of a hidden and faithful providence.
section.frameNote: One dark scene in three beats: the flight from Bethlehem to Moab; the death of Elimelech; and the death of both sons, leaving Naomi bereft.

units.label: Famine drives a family to Moab

verses.text: In the days when the judges ruled, there was a {{famine|famine}} in the land. And a certain man from {{bethlehem|Bethlehem}} in Judah, with his wife and two sons, went to reside in the land of Moab.

The man’s name was Elimelech, his wife’s name was {{naomi|Naomi}}, and the names of his two sons were Mahlon and Chilion. They were Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah, and they entered the land of Moab and settled there.
Then Naomi’s husband Elimelech died, and she was left with her two sons,
who took Moabite women as their wives, one named Orpah and the other named Ruth. And after they had lived in Moab about ten years,
both Mahlon and Chilion also died, and Naomi was left without her two sons and without her husband.

famine.gloss: Famine, hunger, a failure of food. In the covenant, famine could signal God's discipline on a wayward people, Deuteronomy 28. Here it drives an Israelite family out of the promised land in search of bread, setting the whole story in motion.

bethlehem.gloss: House of bread. The name carries a sharp irony: a famine empties the house of bread, so the family leaves it for Moab. In time the true bread will come from Bethlehem, first the line of David, and at last Jesus, the bread of life, Micah 5:2 and John 6:35.

naomi.gloss: Pleasant, lovely, sweet. Her name frames the tragedy of the book: the pleasant one is stripped of husband and sons and will soon ask to be called Mara, bitter. The movement from full to empty and back again drives the whole of chapter one.

basic.context: The book of Ruth is set in the days of the judges, a dark and lawless time when everyone did as they saw fit. A famine hits Bethlehem, and a man named Elimelech takes his wife Naomi and their two sons to live in Moab, a foreign and often hostile land. There the father dies, the sons marry Moabite women, and then both sons die too, leaving Naomi with nothing.
basic.hermeneutics: This is history told with great economy: five verses cover ten years and three deaths. The narrator does not scold Elimelech directly, so readers debate whether leaving Bethlehem was faithless or simply desperate. Either way, the point is the depth of Naomi's loss and the quiet truth that God has not disappeared, even when life looks like pure disaster.
basic.originalLanguages: The famine, ra'ab, empties Bethlehem, whose name means house of bread. Naomi's name means pleasant, which makes her coming grief all the sharper. These names are not decoration; they set up the reversals the whole book will trace.
basic.typology: Naomi's emptying, a journey out of the land into exile and loss, echoes Israel's own story and points ahead to the pattern of death before resurrection. The empty womb and empty home will be filled by a redeemer, a shadow of the greater emptying and filling accomplished in Christ.
basic.christ: Bethlehem, the house of bread, is where this sorrow begins, and it is where the Saviour will one day be born. The famine and death that empty Naomi begin a story that runs straight to David and on to Jesus, showing that God was weaving redemption even through the family's darkest days.
basic.god: Though God is never named as acting in these verses, He is the unseen author of the whole account. We learn that God's providence is often hidden inside suffering, that He does not abandon His people in their grief, and that He can begin a work of rescue in what looks like the end of everything.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 21 sets the lawless backdrop. Genesis 12 and 26 show earlier famines that tested faith. Micah 5 promises a ruler from Bethlehem, and Matthew 1 will place Ruth in that very line.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's big story of a God who redeems the lost begins here in miniature: an empty, grieving woman in a foreign land. From this bitter start God will bring a kinsman-redeemer, a royal line, and finally the Christ, proving that no darkness is beyond the reach of His quiet, faithful purpose.

context.literary: The opening scene of a four-act story, told with striking compression. Names, places, and deaths are stacked quickly to establish the setting and the crisis. The repeated language of leaving and being left, and the movement from Bethlehem to Moab, prepares the return that dominates the next movement.
context.historical: The days of the judges were marked by covenant unfaithfulness, invasion, and social chaos, summed up in Judges by the refrain that everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Moab was a traditional enemy of Israel and a place of pagan worship, so settling there was no small step. A famine in the land was often read as covenant discipline.
context.canonical: Ruth follows Judges and precedes Samuel, bridging the chaos of the judges and the rise of the monarchy. This bleak opening plants the family whose line will lead to David, and through David to the Messiah, giving a small domestic tragedy vast redemptive significance.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the setting of loss and emptiness against which God's hidden faithfulness will shine, and to introduce Naomi, whose journey from full to empty to full again structures the book.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: These verses describe a family's decisions and disasters; they do not prescribe fleeing to Moab or marrying outside the covenant. The abiding lessons are the reality of suffering in a fallen world and the quiet presence of God's providence within it.

debates.question: Was Elimelech's move to Moab a sinful failure of faith or an understandable act of survival?

views.label: A faithless departure
views.case: He left the promised land and the house of bread for a pagan enemy nation, and his sons then married Moabite women; the string of deaths that follows is often read as the sad fruit of leaving the place of covenant blessing.

views.label: An understandable flight from famine
views.case: The text never openly condemns him; the patriarchs too moved during famine, and the narrator's restraint suggests the focus is on Naomi's suffering and God's providence, not on assigning blame.

debates.bearing: Either reading leaves the main point intact: real loss unfolds in a broken world, and God is quietly at work to redeem it, whatever the mix of human fault and circumstance.

study.originalLanguages: The scene turns on names and their meanings. A famine, ra'ab, empties Bethlehem, the house of bread, forcing the family out; the very name of the town mocks their hunger. Naomi means pleasant, a name that will feel unbearable to her once she is stripped of husband and sons, so that in the next movement she asks to be called Mara, bitter. The narrator lets these names do the theological work, setting up a story of emptiness that God will reverse.

typology.type: Naomi emptied and exiled from the land
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the pattern of loss before restoration that runs through Scripture and climaxes in the death and resurrection of Christ.

typology.type: Bethlehem, the house of bread struck by famine
typology.fulfillment: Answered when Bethlehem gives the world the bread of life, Jesus, born in David's town, John 6:35 and Micah 5:2.

study.christ: The trail to Christ begins in these bitter verses. This is the family of Elimelech from Bethlehem, and their story will run through Boaz and Obed to Jesse and David, and at last to Jesus, Matthew 1:5-6. The house of bread, emptied here by famine, will one day be filled by the Saviour born there. Even the emptying of Naomi anticipates the gospel shape of death giving way to resurrection life, the pattern perfected in Christ.
study.god: God is not named as acting in these five verses, and that silence is the point: His hand is hidden, not absent. We learn that the LORD's providence often runs underneath suffering rather than around it, that He does not forsake His people even when every visible support is stripped away, and that He is already at work to redeem what looks like sheer ruin. The God who seems distant in Naomi's grief is the same God quietly steering her toward rescue.

crossReferences.note: In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes, the lawless backdrop to Ruth.
Abram too went down during a famine, an earlier test of faith when the land failed to provide.
Bethlehem, small among the clans of Judah, will bring forth the ruler of Israel.
This family line runs through Boaz and Ruth to David, and on to Jesus the Messiah.

study.oneStory: The great story of redemption begins here in the smallest, saddest key: a grieving widow, empty in a foreign land. Yet Bethlehem, the house of bread, and the family of Elimelech are exactly where God has chosen to work. From this bitter providence He will raise up a kinsman-redeemer, a king, and finally the Christ, showing that His purposes advance even through famine, exile, and death.

sermon.title: “Can This Be Naomi?”
sermon.why: The opening message of Begg's expository series A Study in Ruth, working from Ruth 1:1 through the family's flight to Moab and the deaths that empty Naomi, showing God's providence at work in the ordinary and the tragic. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement's setting and losses.

quiz.q: What time period is the book of Ruth set in?

options.text: The days when the judges ruled, a lawless and unsettled era in Israel.
options.note: Ruth 1:1 places the story in the turbulent days of the judges.

options.text: The reign of King David.
options.note: David is the goal the book points to, but the events happen before his reign, in the judges era.

options.text: The years of Israel's exile in Babylon.
options.note: That exile is centuries later; Ruth is set in the judges period.

options.text: The time of Moses and the wilderness wandering.
options.note: That is much earlier; Ruth belongs to the settled but chaotic judges era.

quiz.q: Why did Elimelech take his family to Moab?

options.text: A famine had struck the land, and he sought food and survival abroad.
options.note: Ruth 1:1 names the famine as the reason for the move.

options.text: God commanded him to settle in Moab.
options.note: There is no such command; the move was driven by famine, not a divine call.

options.text: He was fleeing a war in Bethlehem.
options.note: The text names famine, not war, as the cause.

options.text: He wanted to marry his sons to Moabite women.
options.note: The marriages came later; the move itself was for food during the famine.

quiz.q: What is significant about the name Bethlehem in this opening?

options.text: It means house of bread, so a famine there is a bitter irony that drives the family away.
options.note: The house of bread is emptied by famine, and later filled by the bread of life.

options.text: It means city of David and honors the coming king.
options.note: Bethlehem does become David's town, but the name means house of bread, which is the irony here.

options.text: It was the capital of Moab.
options.note: Bethlehem is in Judah, not Moab; Moab is where they flee to.

options.text: It has no meaning worth noting.
options.note: The name is loaded with meaning that the narrator uses on purpose.

quiz.q: By the end of these verses, what has happened to Naomi?

options.text: She has lost her husband and both sons and is left empty in a foreign land.
options.note: Ruth 1:5 states she was left without her two sons and without her husband.

options.text: She has become wealthy in Moab.
options.note: The opposite is true; she is emptied of family, not filled with wealth.

options.text: She has returned safely to Bethlehem.
options.note: That return comes in the next movement; here she is still in Moab and bereaved.

options.text: She has remarried a Moabite man.
options.note: There is no remarriage; she is left a grieving widow.

quiz.q: Where is God in these five verses of loss?

options.text: He is unnamed but not absent; His providence is quietly at work beneath the suffering.
options.note: The hidden hand of God is a central theme of Ruth from the very start.

options.text: He has abandoned the family because of their sin.
options.note: The family suffers, but the book will show God has not abandoned them at all.

options.text: He is actively speaking and commanding throughout the scene.
options.note: God is strikingly silent here; His work is hidden, not spoken.

options.text: He is powerless to help in such a dark time.
options.note: The book reveals God as sovereign over every event, not powerless.

questions: The story opens in a lawless, famine-struck time, yet God is quietly at work. Where in your own hard season might God be present in ways you cannot yet see?
Elimelech left the house of bread for Moab when the pressure came. When trouble hits, are you tempted to run from the place of faith and provision rather than trust God within it?
Naomi lost her husband and both sons in quick succession. How do you hold on to God's goodness when loss piles on loss?
The narrator does not rush to explain or justify the suffering. How does it help to know the Bible tells the truth about grief without tidy answers?
This bitter beginning is the start of a redemption story that leads to Christ. How does knowing the ending change the way you read your own painful chapters?

translationNotes.note: Bethlehem means house of bread, a pointed irony since a famine there is what drives the family out; the same town will later be David's home and the birthplace of Christ.
Ephrathites refers to the clan associated with the region of Bethlehem, also called Ephrathah, tying this family to the promise of Micah 5:2.
The Hebrew stresses that Naomi was left, an emptying that sets up her lament in verses 20 and 21 and the whole book's reversal from empty to full.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 1:1-5. The clause order in verse 4 follows the BSB, which places the note about ten years in Moab within that verse.
